channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: McKinsey Says $1 Trillion In Sales Will Go Through AI Agents. Most Businesses Are Invisible.
executive_summary: McKinsey predicts that $1 trillion in sales will flow through AI agents, but businesses must first overhaul their data architecture to make it compatible with these agents. The current anti-bot systems, developed over the last 20 years, are obstructing access to valuable customers. Companies that delay adapting their data stacks risk falling behind as the AI ecosystem evolves rapidly.
bullets: McKinsey forecasts that $1 trillion in sales will go through AI agents, highlighting a massive shift in commerce. This projection underscores the urgency for businesses to adapt or risk losing market share. It's like a tidal wave approaching the shore; those who don't prepare will be swept away.

Twenty years of anti-bot architecture now blocks access to your best customers. This outdated design prevents AI agents from effectively interacting with potential buyers, making it crucial for companies to rethink their data systems. Imagine trying to enter a club where the bouncer only lets in certain types of guests — if your data isn't dressed right, it won't get in.

OpenClaw, a proposed personal AI agent, will only function if companies completely rebuild their data architecture to be agent-readable and agent-writable. This means businesses need to transform their data into a format that AI can understand and manipulate. Think of it like translating a book into a new language; if the translation is poor, the story gets lost.

Wrapping an API in a Multi-Channel Platform (MCP) is insufficient for real agent access. This approach may seem convenient, but it doesn't provide the depth of interaction that AI agents require. It's like putting a fancy cover on a book without ensuring the pages inside are well-written.

Stripe's challenges with its Sigma tool reveal the complexities of data access for AI agents. The depth of this challenge shows that even established companies struggle to adapt their systems for AI. Imagine a chef trying to cook a gourmet meal with only half the ingredients; the result will be far from satisfactory.

SAP's data architecture has a significant gap, referred to as the 'Grand Canyon gap.' This metaphor illustrates the vast distance between current capabilities and what is needed for effective AI integration. It’s like trying to cross a wide canyon on a tightrope; one misstep could lead to disaster.

One misconception is that agent discovery is similar to SEO. This misunderstanding can lead companies to misallocate resources, thinking they can simply optimize their data for AI agents like they would for search engines. It's like assuming a map will guide you to a treasure without knowing the terrain — you need more than just directions.

Another misconception is that schemas only work for simple products. This belief can limit the complexity of data structures needed for AI agents to function effectively. Think of it like trying to build a skyscraper with only blueprints for a single-story house; the foundation won't hold.

Many believe that customers won't trust AI agents. This misconception can hinder the adoption of AI technologies, as businesses may hesitate to implement them. It's like fearing a new bridge will collapse without ever testing its strength; you might miss out on a safer, faster route.

The belief that a 'wait and see' approach is viable can be detrimental. Companies that delay action while competitors enhance their data stacks are essentially signing their own death warrants. It's like watching a race from the sidelines while others sprint ahead; by the time you decide to join, it may be too late.

Eighty percent of meaningful information remains trapped in tribal knowledge within organizations. This hidden knowledge can prevent effective AI integration, as it isn't documented in accessible formats. Imagine trying to solve a puzzle without knowing where the pieces are; you'll struggle to see the full picture.

To succeed, businesses should build their systems for AI agents first, with human users following. This approach ensures that the architecture is optimized for AI functionality, leading to better outcomes. It’s like constructing a highway designed for heavy traffic before allowing regular cars on it; the infrastructure needs to be robust.
key_takeaways: Rebuild your data architecture to be agent-readable and agent-writable immediately. This is crucial for accessing the $1 trillion market projected by McKinsey. Delaying this transformation could result in losing competitive advantage as the AI landscape evolves.

Set a deadline to address the 'Grand Canyon gap' in your data systems within the next quarter. This will help ensure that your company can effectively integrate AI agents before competitors do. If you wait too long, you risk being left behind in a rapidly changing market.

Establish a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must focus on data accessibility for AI agents, not just functionality. This shift in focus will help your team prioritize the right aspects of data architecture. If you neglect this, you may end up with systems that fail to leverage AI effectively.

technical_terms.OpenClaw: A proposed personal AI agent that requires companies to overhaul their data systems to be compatible. Think of it like a new smartphone that only works with the latest operating system; if your old phone can't update, it won't function.
technical_terms.API: An Application Programming Interface that allows different software systems to communicate. Think of it like a waiter taking your order at a restaurant; the waiter relays your requests to the kitchen and brings back your food.
technical_terms.MCP: A Multi-Channel Platform that integrates various communication channels but may not provide full access for AI agents. Think of it like a multi-lane highway that still has toll booths blocking some vehicles from passing freely.
technical_terms.Sigma: A tool from Stripe that faces challenges in providing deep data access for AI agents. Think of it like a key that only opens some doors in a house; it might not unlock the most important rooms.
technical_terms.Grand Canyon gap: A metaphor describing the significant distance between current data capabilities and what is needed for effective AI integration. Think of it like trying to jump across a wide canyon; without the right tools, you won't make it.
technical_terms.SEO: Search Engine Optimization, a method to improve website visibility in search engines. Think of it like polishing a trophy to make it shine; it looks good, but if the trophy isn't valuable, the shine won't matter.
technical_terms.Schemas: Structured formats for organizing data that help AI agents understand complex information. Think of it like a filing cabinet with labeled folders; without proper organization, finding the right document becomes a challenge.
technical_terms.Tribal knowledge: Informal knowledge held by employees that isn't documented or shared widely. Think of it like a family recipe passed down orally; if no one writes it down, it may be lost to future generations.
technical_terms.Data architecture: The structural design of an organization's data systems, crucial for AI functionality. Think of it like the blueprint of a building; if the design is flawed, the entire structure may collapse.
technical_terms.Agent-readable: Data that can be easily understood and processed by AI agents. Think of it like a book written in a language the reader understands; if the language is foreign, the reader won't grasp the content.
